class_schedule.topic: Understanding the Consumer: Psychology Meets Digital
learning_objectives: Define consumer behavior and explain its relevance to digital communications professionals
Identify the psychological, social, and cultural factors that influence consumer decisions
Map the consumer decision-making process and its digital touchpoints
readings: Kahneman, Thinking, Fast and Slow, Ch. 1-3 (selected sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every digital communication is designed to influence someone to think, feel, or do something. Consumer behavior is the scientific study of why people make the choices they do. Understanding these principles makes you a more effective and more ethical communicator.
key_concepts: Consumer behavior defined: the study of how individuals make decisions to spend their resources (time, money, attention) on consumption-related items
The consumer decision process: problem recognition, information search, evaluation of alternatives, purchase decision, post-purchase evaluation
Psychological influences: motivation, perception, learning, memory, attitudes
Social influences: reference groups, family, opinion leaders, social class
Digital transformation of behavior: how digital tools and platforms have changed each stage of the decision process
lecture_notes.demonstration: Tracing a real consumer's decision journey for a significant purchase (new laptop), mapping every digital touchpoint from initial Google search through review sites, social media, comparison tools, and final purchase, identifying the influences at each stage
setup_requirements: Think with Google consumer insights tools

review.value: Kahneman's framework: System 1 (fast, automatic, emotional) and System 2 (slow, deliberate, rational); most consumer decisions are driven by System 1, which is why emotional appeals in communications are often more effective than logical arguments

The traditional linear funnel (awareness > consideration > purchase) has been replaced by a messy, non-linear journey with multiple touchpoints, social influence, and instant access to information at every stage

class_schedule.topic: Perception, Attention, and the Psychology of Persuasion
learning_objectives: Apply principles of selective attention and perception to digital content design
Use Cialdini's principles of influence ethically in communications
Evaluate cognitive biases that affect how audiences process digital messages
readings: Cialdini, Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: People do not process information objectively. They filter it through perception, bias, and mental shortcuts. Understanding these psychological principles allows you to create communications that are noticed, processed, and remembered, ethically.
key_concepts: Selective attention: people notice only a fraction of the stimuli they encounter; your content competes for limited cognitive bandwidth
Cialdini's six principles: reciprocity, commitment/consistency, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity
Cognitive biases in digital: anchoring, confirmation bias, bandwagon effect, loss aversion, the framing effect
Ethical persuasion vs. manipulation: using psychology to help people make good decisions vs. exploiting biases
Application to digital: how each principle manifests in social media, email, advertising, and web design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Identifying Cialdini's principles in 10 real digital communications examples (ads, social posts, landing pages, emails) and evaluating each on the ethical persuasion spectrum

review.value: People look to others' behavior to determine their own, especially under uncertainty; testimonials, user counts, reviews, and 'trending' labels leverage social proof. Ethical use presents genuine social proof; unethical use fabricates it.

People feel the pain of losing something more strongly than the pleasure of gaining something equivalent; 'Don't miss out' framing is more motivating than 'You could gain' framing, which is why urgency and scarcity are powerful but must be used honestly

learning_objectives: Apply Maslow's hierarchy and other motivational theories to understand consumer needs
Develop psychographic segmentation schemes based on values, lifestyles, and motivations
Connect motivational insights to messaging strategy and content development
readings: VALS (Values, Attitudes, and Lifestyles) framework documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: People do not buy products. They buy solutions to problems, fulfillment of desires, and expressions of identity. Understanding what motivates your audience at a deep level, beyond demographics, enables communications that resonate emotionally and drive action.
key_concepts: Maslow's hierarchy applied to communications: physiological, safety, belonging, esteem, self-actualization as message frameworks
Motivational theories: intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation, hedonic vs. utilitarian consumption
Psychographic segmentation: VALS framework, lifestyle segments, values-based clustering
Consumer identity: how purchases and brand affiliations express and construct self-identity
Connecting motivation to message: matching your communications to the audience's underlying needs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Applying Maslow's hierarchy to reposition a product's messaging: taking a home security system and developing five different ad concepts, each targeting a different level of the hierarchy, from basic safety to self-actualization

review.value: Strategic Business Insights' psychographic segmentation system dividing consumers into eight segments based on primary motivation (ideals, achievement, self-expression) and resources; more predictive of consumer behavior than demographics alone
Consumers choose brands and products that align with their actual or aspirational identity; effective communications connect the brand to who the consumer wants to be, not just what they need to buy

class_schedule.topic: Social Influence and Digital Word of Mouth
learning_objectives: Analyze how social influence operates in digital environments
Evaluate the power and mechanisms of online word of mouth (eWOM) and reviews
Apply social influence theory to influencer marketing and community-building strategies
readings: Berger, Contagious: Why Things Catch On, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Consumer decisions are profoundly social. People look to friends, family, influencers, and strangers online before making choices. Understanding how social influence operates in digital environments is essential for any communications professional working in marketing, PR, or content.
key_concepts: Reference groups: aspirational, membership, and dissociative groups and their influence on consumption
Opinion leaders and influencers: individuals who disproportionately influence others' attitudes and behaviors
eWOM (electronic word of mouth): online reviews, social media recommendations, and their outsized impact
Berger's STEPPS framework: Social currency, Triggers, Emotion, Public, Practical value, Stories
Viral content psychology: why people share content and how to design for shareability ethically
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing three viral marketing campaigns through Berger's STEPPS framework, identifying which principles each leveraged and evaluating the ethical implications of the psychological techniques used

review.value: Berger's six principles of contagiousness: Social currency (makes the sharer look good), Triggers (top-of-mind associations), Emotion (high-arousal emotions drive sharing), Public (visible behavior is imitated), Practical value (useful information is shared), Stories (narratives carry messages)

Online reviews and recommendations from peers are trusted 12x more than brand marketing; a single negative review can reduce purchase probability by 22%, making reputation management and genuine customer experience the foundation of digital marketing

learning_objectives: Apply behavioral economics concepts to digital communications and UX design
Evaluate the ethical boundaries of choice architecture and nudging
Design communications that facilitate good consumer decisions rather than exploiting biases

lecture_notes.introduction: Every digital interface is a choice environment. The way options are presented, the defaults that are set, and the information that is highlighted all influence decisions. Understanding choice architecture gives communicators enormous power, and enormous ethical responsibility.
key_concepts: Choice architecture: how the design of choice environments influences decisions
Nudging: designing environments that guide people toward better decisions without restricting freedom
Default effect: the power of pre-selected options (opt-in vs. opt-out has massive behavioral impact)
Decision fatigue: how too many choices lead to worse decisions or no decision at all
Ethical choice architecture: designing for the consumer's benefit vs. designing for conversion at the consumer's expense
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesigning three digital experiences (subscription page, cookie consent, product comparison) to apply ethical choice architecture: maintaining business objectives while genuinely serving consumer interests

review.value: Pre-selected options are accepted by 80-90% of people; this is why opt-out organ donation countries have dramatically higher donation rates than opt-in countries. In digital communications, default settings in email preferences, privacy settings, and subscription plans profoundly shape consumer behavior.

A nudge is ethical when it: (1) benefits the person being nudged, (2) is transparent rather than hidden, (3) preserves freedom of choice, and (4) would be endorsed by the person if they understood the nudge. Dark patterns fail all four tests.

questions.question: Explain Kahneman's System 1 and System 2 thinking, and describe how a social media advertiser would design content that appeals to each system.
questions.answer: System 1 is fast, automatic, emotional, and intuitive; it handles most daily decisions without conscious effort. System 2 is slow, deliberate, logical, and effortful; it is activated for complex or important decisions. For System 1: a social media ad would use striking visuals, emotional storytelling, social proof (showing happy customers), and simple, bold claims that create an immediate positive feeling. For System 2: a retargeting ad for someone actively comparing products would present detailed specifications, comparison charts, expert reviews, and logical arguments that support careful evaluation. Most social media advertising targets System 1 because scrolling behavior is System 1 by nature.

questions.question: Choose two of Cialdini's six principles of influence and explain how each could be applied ethically vs. unethically in email marketing.
questions.answer: Scarcity - Ethical: 'Only 15 spots remain in our March workshop' when there genuinely are only 15 spots. This helps interested people act before the opportunity disappears. Unethical: 'Only 15 spots remain!' when there is no limit and the number resets every day. This manufactures urgency through deception. Social Proof - Ethical: 'Join 50,000 subscribers who get our weekly marketing tips' when the subscriber count is accurate. This helps the reader assess whether the newsletter is valued by peers. Unethical: Displaying fabricated subscriber counts or fake testimonials from people who never used the product. This exploits the trust mechanism underlying social proof.

rubric.key: Selects two principles and defines them correctly
Provides convincing ethical application for each
Provides convincing unethical application with clear contrast

questions.question: Describe Berger's STEPPS framework and apply one principle to explain why a specific piece of content you have seen went viral.
questions.answer: STEPPS: Social currency (makes sharer look good), Triggers (environmental cues prompt thinking about it), Emotion (high-arousal emotions drive sharing), Public (visible behavior is imitated), Practical value (useful information is shared), Stories (narratives carry messages). Application example using Practical value: Infographics showing 'How to fold a fitted sheet' or '5 keyboard shortcuts that save 30 minutes a day' go viral because they offer immediately useful information. People share them to help their network (also gaining social currency as someone who shares useful content). The practical value principle explains why educational and how-to content consistently outperforms purely entertaining content in share rates.

rubric.key: Applies one principle to a specific viral example

questions.question: What is the default effect, and describe an ethical concern with using default settings in digital subscription sign-ups.
questions.answer: The default effect is the tendency for people to accept pre-selected options rather than actively changing them, regardless of their actual preference. In subscription sign-ups, an ethical concern: pre-selecting the most expensive plan as the default exploits decision fatigue and the default effect to push consumers into spending more than they intended. When the default is an annual subscription auto-renewing with the most features at the highest price, and the cheaper monthly option requires several clicks to find, the design prioritizes revenue extraction over consumer choice. Ethical practice would pre-select the most popular plan based on actual user data, make all options equally visible, and clearly explain the differences.

major_assignments.title: Consumer Behavior Analysis and Campaign Recommendation
major_assignments.description: Select a brand and analyze its target consumers using consumer behavior frameworks. Develop a communications campaign recommendation grounded in psychological and behavioral insights. The campaign must apply at least three consumer behavior principles ethically.
objectives: Analyze a target consumer segment using psychological and behavioral frameworks
Apply consumer behavior principles to develop communications strategy
Design campaign concepts grounded in behavioral science
Evaluate the ethical implications of all psychological techniques used
deliverables: Consumer behavior analysis (8-10 pages) applying course frameworks to a real consumer segment
Psychographic profile with motivational analysis beyond basic demographics
Campaign recommendation applying at least three behavioral principles with ethical justification
Three creative concepts (ad, email, social post) demonstrating behavioral principles in practice
Ethics evaluation assessing each psychological technique used against ethical standards

criteria: Consumer segment is analyzed using multiple frameworks (Maslow, VALS, Cialdini)
Analysis goes beyond demographics to genuine psychographic and behavioral insights
Behavioral patterns are supported by evidence or research
Insights directly inform the campaign recommendation
Campaign applies at least three behavioral principles correctly
Strategy connects consumer insights to specific messaging and channel decisions
Creative concepts demonstrate the principles in practice
Campaign is realistic and executable
Each psychological technique is evaluated against ethical standards
Ethical line between persuasion and manipulation is articulated clearly
Alternative approaches are considered for any borderline techniques
Ethics evaluation demonstrates genuine critical thinking
Written work is clear, well-organized, and professionally formatted
Creative concepts are presented with sufficient detail
Analysis and recommendations are logically connected
All claims are supported with evidence or reasoning
Deep consumer behavior analysis using multiple frameworks
Strategic campaign grounded in behavioral science principles
Rigorous ethical evaluation of all techniques used
Professional presentation connecting analysis to actionable recommendations

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on consumer behavior concepts and applications

weekly_analyses.description: Weekly consumer behavior observation and analysis exercises

major_assignment.description: Consumer behavior analysis and ethically-grounded campaign recommendation

midterm_examination.description: Written examination on psychological principles and consumer decision processes

final_examination.description: Cumulative examination including behavioral analysis and ethical evaluation

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Behavioral analysis discussions require participation. More than 3 absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Consumer analysis exercises submitted more than 5 days late will not be accepted.
course_policies.collaboration: Behavioral observations may be discussed in class. All written analyses and campaign recommendations are individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Consumer research must cite sources. Behavioral claims must be supported by evidence. Fabricating consumer data or research findings is academic fraud.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need internet access for research, a laptop for in-class analysis, and access to consumer insight tools (Think with Google, VALS survey). No specialized software is required.
